tagged_story_for_tts:
First contact.<[silence:90]> Misunderstood rituals.<[silence:90]>

The title colombus_and_other_cannibals comes from a 1978 book by Jack D. Forbes, flipping the script:<[silence:65]> What if Europeans were the real "cannibals"—consuming lands, cultures, and lives—while Indigenous peoples practiced sacred, symbolic acts they called "eating"?

When Columbus landed, the Taíno offered guanín—gold-dust rituals to honor spirits.<[silence:90]> He saw treasure.<[silence:90]> When Carib warriors ate slivers of enemies in battle rites (never for hunger), Spanish chroniclers screamed "savages!" Yet Europe’s own elites drank blood in "medicinal" tonics until the 1800s.<[silence:90]>

The Aztec tlacatlaolli—warrior feasts with captured foes—wasn’t about meals.<[silence:90]> It was theater:<[silence:65]> a debt to the sun god Huitzilopochtli, who "fed" on human energy to keep the world turning.<[silence:90]> Meanwhile, conquistadors ate entire civilizations:<[silence:65]> enslaving 90% of the Taíno in 25 years, mining their bones for lime.<[silence:90]>

Forbes’ point? "Cannibal" was the ultimate slander—a mirror.<[silence:90]> The real hunger wasn’t for flesh, but for power disguised as piety.<[silence:90]>

Three related subjects are ritual versus taboo, language as conquest weapon, and how empires rewrite history.<[silence:90]>
clean_story:
First contact. Misunderstood rituals.

The title colombus_and_other_cannibals comes from a 1978 book by Jack D. Forbes, flipping the script: What if Europeans were the real "cannibals"—consuming lands, cultures, and lives—while Indigenous peoples practiced sacred, symbolic acts they called "eating"?

When Columbus landed, the Taíno offered guanín—gold-dust rituals to honor spirits. He saw treasure. When Carib warriors ate slivers of enemies in battle rites (never for hunger), Spanish chroniclers screamed "savages!" Yet Europe’s own elites drank blood in "medicinal" tonics until the 1800s.

The Aztec tlacatlaolli—warrior feasts with captured foes—wasn’t about meals. It was theater: a debt to the sun god Huitzilopochtli, who "fed" on human energy to keep the world turning. Meanwhile, conquistadors ate entire civilizations: enslaving 90% of the Taíno in 25 years, mining their bones for lime.

Forbes’ point? "Cannibal" was the ultimate slander—a mirror. The real hunger wasn’t for flesh, but for power disguised as piety.

Three related subjects are ritual versus taboo, language as conquest weapon, and how empires rewrite history.
